title: Getting Started with ipf
text: Survey samples rarely match population perfectly key demographics. Raking (iterative proportional fitting) adjusts case weights weighted sample margins match known population targets. ipf makes fast straightforward, Rust computational core clean R interface. vignette walks complete raking workflow using bundled anes24 dataset — subset 2024 American National Election Study.

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: writing targets, inspect levels sample see values missing: define targets, target names must match data values exactly. NA values ignored variable raking.
code: table(anes24$sex, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #> Female   Male   <NA>  #>    503    458      5 table(anes24$race, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #>    Asian    Black Hispanic    Other    White     <NA>  #>       28      118      114       63      632       11 table(anes24$income, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #> $50k-$100k Over $100k Under $50k       <NA>  #>        300        389        230         47 table(anes24$region, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #>   Midwest Northeast     South      West      <NA>  #>       209       118       319       214       106  colSums(is.na(anes24[c('sex', 'race', 'income', 'region')])) #>    sex   race income region  #>      5     11     47    106

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: Targets named list named numeric vectors. vector’s names must match levels data, values proportions summing 1. targets don’t sum 1, ipf normalize automatically warning.
code: targets <- list(   sex = c(Male = 0.472, Female = 0.528),   race = c(     White = 0.706,     Black = 0.121,     Hispanic = 0.107,     Asian = 0.047,     Other = 0.019   ),   income = c(     'Under $50k' = 0.151,     '$50k-$100k' = 0.294,     'Over $100k' = 0.555   ) )

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: main function rake(): ’s — result ipf_rake object containing weights diagnostics. already design weights, pass base_weights:
code: result <- rake(anes24, targets, cap = NULL) result #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf) #> Converged: Yes (695 iterations, max prop err = 9.83e-07) #> Variables raked: "sex", "race", and "income" #> Design effect: 1.176 | Effective n: 822 / 966 #> Weight range: [0.05, 3.793] | Mean: 1.01 | SD: 0.424 base_w <- ifelse(anes24$sex == 'Female', 1.1, 0.9) base_w[is.na(base_w)] <- 1  base_weighted <- rake(anes24, targets, base_weights = base_w, cap = NULL) base_weighted #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf) #> Converged: Yes (696 iterations, max prop err = 9.87e-07) #> Variables raked: "sex", "race", and "income" #> Design effect: 1.176 | Effective n: 822 / 966 #> Weight range: [0.05, 3.788] | Mean: 1.01 | SD: 0.424

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: design effect measures much weighting inflates variance. deff 1.0 means inflation (uniform weights); higher values mean less effective data.
code: design_effect(result$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.175752 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 821.602

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: programmatic use, broom-style methods return tibbles:
code: # One row per variable-level tidy(result) #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>    variable level      target weighted_pct discrepancy #>    <chr>    <chr>       <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl> #>  1 sex      Male        0.472       0.472    -2.22e- 9 #>  2 sex      Female      0.528       0.528     2.22e- 9 #>  3 race     White       0.706       0.706     5.71e-10 #>  4 race     Black       0.121       0.121     1.61e-12 #>  5 race     Hispanic    0.107       0.107    -5.71e-10 #>  6 race     Asian       0.047       0.0470   -1.22e-10 #>  7 race     Other       0.019       0.0190    1.20e-10 #>  8 income   Under $50k  0.151       0.151     7.22e-16 #>  9 income   $50k-$100k  0.294       0.294     1.72e-15 #> 10 income   Over $100k  0.555       0.555    -6.66e-16  # One-row summary glance(result) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   converged iterations max_prop_err  deff n_eff n_obs n_vars #>   <lgl>          <int>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int>  <int> #> 1 TRUE             695  0.000000983  1.18  822.   966      3

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: use weights downstream analyses, attach data: .weight column can used downstream analyses. regional distribution weighting:
code: weighted_data <- augment(result) head(weighted_data) #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   state sex    race  income     education    married presidential region .weight #>   <chr> <chr>  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>   <chr>        <chr>    <dbl> #> 1 CO    Female White Over $100k NA           Divorc… Trump        West     1.45  #> 2 CA    Male   White Over $100k NA           Married Harris       West     1.32  #> 3 NC    Female White NA         Bachelor's   Married Harris       South    0.172 #> 4 MA    Male   NA    Over $100k Some college Married Trump        North…   1.45  #> 5 WA    Female Black NA         Bachelor's   Married NA           West     0.183 #> 6 GA    Male   White $50k-$100k NA           NA      Trump        South    0.935 unweighted_region <- prop.table(table(anes24$region))  weighted_region <- with(   weighted_data[!is.na(weighted_data$region), ],   tapply(.weight, region, sum) ) weighted_region <- weighted_region / sum(weighted_region)  rbind(   unweighted = round(unweighted_region[names(weighted_region)], 3),   weighted = round(weighted_region, 3) ) #>            Midwest Northeast South  West #> unweighted   0.243     0.137 0.371 0.249 #> weighted     0.244     0.143 0.358 0.254

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: default, weights capped 5. Tighter bounds reduce extreme weights can leave residual mismatch.
code: # Unbounded fit from above range(result$weights) #> [1] 0.05047476 3.79304942 design_effect(result$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.175752 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 821.602  # Default cap default_bounded <- rake(anes24, targets) range(default_bounded$weights) #> [1] 0.05047476 3.79304942 design_effect(default_bounded$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.175752 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 821.602  # Tighter cap tight <- rake(anes24, targets, cap = 3) range(tight$weights) #> [1] 0.04148087 2.99609812 design_effect(tight$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.172102 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 824.1607  # Or specify both min and max bounds bounded <- rake(anes24, targets, bounds = c(0.3, 3)) range(bounded$weights) #> [1] 0.300000 2.101607

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: many potential raking variables, can let ipf select discrepant ones: Use type = "nlim" select top N discrepant variables, iterate = TRUE re-check round add newly discrepant variables.
code: targets_many <- list(   sex = c(Male = 0.472, Female = 0.528),   race = c(     White = 0.706,     Black = 0.121,     Hispanic = 0.107,     Asian = 0.047,     Other = 0.019   ),   income = c('Under $50k' = 0.151, '$50k-$100k' = 0.294, 'Over $100k' = 0.555),   married = c(     Married = 0.58,     Widowed = 0.06,     Divorced = 0.10,     Separated = 0.01,     'Never married' = 0.25   ) )  # Only rake on variables where discrepancy exceeds 5% result_pct <- rake(anes24, targets_many, type = 'pctlim', pctlim = 0.05) result_pct$vars_used #> [1] "race"    "income"  "married"

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: can inspect raw discrepancy scores without raking:
code: find_discrepant_vars(   anes24,   targets_many,   weights = rep(1, nrow(anes24)),   choosemethod = 'total' ) #>         sex        race      income     married  #> 0.009173777 0.123801047 0.263427639 0.483570392

title: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend
text: ipf provides fast iterative proportional fitting (raking) survey weighting. computational core written Rust speed numerical stability. supports multiple raking variables, automatic variable selection, weight bounding, comprehensive diagnostics.

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
text: Adjusts survey weights weighted marginal distributions match known population targets. Supports automatic variable selection, iterative re-raking, weight bounding.

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
code: rake(   data,   targets,   base_weights = NULL,   cap = 5,   bounds = NULL,   type = c("nolim", "pctlim", "nlim"),   pctlim = 0.05,   nlim = 5L,   choosemethod = c("total", "max", "average", "totalsquared", "maxsquared",     "averagesquared"),   iterate = FALSE,   max_iter = 1000L,   tol = 1e-06,   verbose = FALSE,   diagnostics_every = 0L )

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
text: data data frame tibble containing survey data. targets named list named numeric vectors specifying target proportions raking variable. Names list must match column names data. vector's names must match levels corresponding variable. Values sum 1 (proportions); , normalized warning. base_weights Optional numeric vector base (design) weights. NULL (default), uniform weights 1 used. Centered mean 1 raking. cap Maximum weight value (ratio cap). Weights exceeding value trimmed weights renormalized. Default 5. Ignored bounds specified. bounds Optional numeric vector length 2, c(lo, hi), specifying minimum maximum weight bounds. Overrides cap. type Variable selection method: "nolim" (default): use variables targets. "pctlim": use variables discrepancy >= pctlim. "nlim": use nlim discrepant variables. pctlim Discrepancy threshold type = "pctlim". Default 0.05 (5 percentage points). nlim Number variables type = "nlim". Default 5. choosemethod Method aggregating per-category discrepancies single variable score. One "total", "max", "average", "totalsquared", "maxsquared", "averagesquared". iterate Logical. TRUE type = "pctlim", re-check discrepancies raking add newly discrepant variables, repeating 10 times. Default FALSE. max_iter Maximum number raking iterations. Default 1000. tol Convergence tolerance (max proportional error). Default 1e-6. verbose Logical. TRUE, print iteration progress. Default FALSE. diagnostics_every Record per-margin diagnostics every k iterations. 0 means baseline. Default 0.

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
text: ipf_rake object (S3 class) containing: weights: final raked weight vector data: input data frame converged: logical iterations: number iterations max_prop_err: final max proportional error targets: normalized targets used vars_used: character vector variables raked base_weights: original base weights type, choosemethod, cap: settings used deff, n_eff: design effect effective sample size diagnostics: tibble per-iteration diagnostics

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)),   age = sample(c('young', 'old'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3)) ) targets <- list(   gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5),   age = c(young = 0.6, old = 0.4) ) result <- rake(data, targets) print(result) #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf)  #> Converged: Yes (3 iterations, max prop err = 5e-08) #> Variables raked: "gender" and "age" #> Design effect: 1.115 | Effective n: 90 / 100 #> Weight range: [0.742, 1.517] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.339
